diagrams.title: DHT Network Architecture
diagrams.description: Shows the peer-to-peer network structure of a Distributed Hash Table with multiple nodes, their unique node IDs, and assigned key ranges arranged in a logical ring topology
elements: Multiple peer nodes (Node A: ID 0010, Node B: ID 0101, Node C: ID 1011, Node D: ID 1110, Node E: ID 0001)
Each node represented as a circle with node ID inside
Nodes arranged in a clockwise ring structure showing logical proximity
Key space represented as a circular hash ring (0 to 2^n-1)
Key ranges shown as arcs between nodes on the ring
Arrows showing which node is responsible for which key range
Content hash values (e.g., Hash(File1) = 0110) placed on the ring
Successor node relationships indicated with directed arrows
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how nodes are organized in a DHT network, how unique node IDs are assigned using hash functions, and how the key space is partitioned among nodes based on their IDs. This establishes the foundation for understanding routing and lookup processes.

diagrams.title: DHT Routing and Lookup Process (Chord Protocol)
diagrams.description: Illustrates the hop-by-hop routing mechanism in a DHT when searching for a key, showing the finger table and routing decisions at each node
elements: Requesting node (Node A) initiating lookup for key K
Finger table at each node showing successor nodes at distances 2^0, 2^1, 2^2, etc.
Intermediate nodes (B, C, D) shown as stepping stones
Final destination node (Node D) holding the requested key
Numbered sequence arrows showing hop-by-hop progression
Routing decision points highlighted at each node
Lookup time complexity shown as O(log n) hops
Labels showing which finger entry is used at each hop
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how DHTs achieve efficient key lookup through progressive routing, learning about finger tables, the exponential skipping technique, and how lookups traverse from source to destination in O(log n) time complexity.

diagrams.title: Kademlia Routing Visualization
diagrams.description: Shows the XOR-based distance metric and tree-structured routing in Kademlia DHT, used by popular systems like BitTorrent and IPFS
elements: Node IDs arranged in a binary tree structure
XOR distance calculation between nodes shown (e.g., ID(0010) XOR ID(1010) = 1000)
K-buckets (k=20) shown as groups of closest nodes
Lookup paths shown as branches of the tree
Node refresh and maintenance operations indicated
Parallel lookup queries shown in multiple branches
Leaf nodes and prefix matching demonstrated
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the Kademlia protocol's unique XOR-based distance metric, how nodes maintain k-buckets of closest contacts, and why this design provides excellent scalability and fault tolerance in practice.

diagrams.title: DHT vs Centralized Storage Architecture
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of traditional centralized client-server model versus decentralized DHT architecture
elements: Left side: Centralized architecture with single server icon connected to multiple clients
Single point of failure highlighted with warning symbol
Right side: DHT architecture with interconnected peer nodes forming mesh
No central server or single point of failure
Scalability arrows showing horizontal scaling vs vertical scaling
Fault tolerance shown with node failure and route rerouting
Data replication paths indicated in DHT
Bandwidth usage comparison labels
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental differences between centralized and distributed systems, recognizing the advantages of DHTs in terms of decentralization, fault tolerance, and scalability, while also understanding trade-offs in complexity and consistency.

diagrams.title: DHT Integration with Blockchain and IPFS
diagrams.description: Shows how Distributed Hash Tables form the backbone of blockchain-compatible decentralized storage systems like IPFS
elements: IPFS logo and framework representation
Content Addressable Storage layer with content hashes
DHT Layer showing peer-to-peer node network
Merkle DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) showing content chunking
Blockchain integration point with smart contracts
User requesting file through IPFS gateway
DHT lookup resolving content hash to peer locations
Data transfer from multiple peers simultaneously
Cryptographic hashing at each stage shown
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the practical application of DHTs in blockchain ecosystems, learning how IPFS uses DHTs for content discovery, how Merkle DAGs enable efficient content addressing, and how this integrates with blockchain for immutable data storage.

charts.title: Major DHT Algorithms Comparison
charts.content: Chord: Uses consistent hashing on a ring, O(log n) lookup, successor-based routing; Kademlia: XOR distance metric, tree topology, O(log n) parallel lookups, used by IPFS/BitTorrent; Pastry: Similar to Kademlia with routing leaf sets, used in PAST; Tapestry: Hierarchical mesh, uses Plaxton routing, good for wide-area systems; CAN: d-dimensional torus, O(d*n^(1/d)) lookup, hash space as multi-dimensional space

charts.title: DHT Characteristics in Blockchain Context
charts.content: Decentralization: No central authority, peer-to-peer data storage; Scalability: Linear scaling with nodes, O(log n) routing complexity; Fault Tolerance: Replication and redundancy, handles node joins/leaves; Load Balancing: Consistent hashing distributes keys evenly; Anonymity: Partial anonymity through distributed architecture; Performance: Trade-off between latency and decentralization; Security: Vulnerable to eclipse attacks, Sybil attacks, needs robust node verification

charts.title: DHT Applications in Decentralized Systems
charts.content: File Sharing: BitTorrent (Kademlia), eDonkey; DNS Alternative: Namecoin, ENS (Ethereum Name Service); Blockchain Storage: IPFS, Filecoin, Swarm; Messaging: Tox, Briar; CDN: S3, Cloudflare distributed storage; Database: Apache Cassandra, DynamoDB-style systems; Content Distribution: WebTorrent, LBRY; Social Platforms: Mastodon ActivityPub backbone

branches: Architecture Components: Peer-to-peer network topology, Node ID generation using hash functions, Key space partitioning (consistent hashing), Routing tables (finger tables, k-buckets), Replication and caching strategies
Algorithms & Protocols: Chord (ring-based), Kademlia (XOR metric), Pastry ( Plaxton routing), Tapestry (hierarchical), CAN (coordinate-based)

Core Properties: Decentralization (no central authority), Scalability (handles millions of nodes), Fault tolerance (replication, redundancy), Load balancing (even key distribution), Anonymity (partial)

Blockchain Integration: IPFS (InterPlanetary File System), Content addressing with hashes, Merkle DAG for data integrity, Filecoin (storage marketplace), Swarm (Ethereum storage), ENS (Ethereum Name Service)

Real-World Applications: BitTorrent (P2P file sharing), Namecoin (decentralized DNS), Ethereum (peer discovery), Bitcoin (DHT for node discovery), Cryptocurrencies (transaction distribution), Messaging apps (decentralized communication)

Challenges: Sybil attacks (fake identities), Eclipse attacks (network partitioning), Scalability vs latency trade-off, Consistency models (CAP theorem), Network churn (node dynamics), Storage efficiency vs redundancy
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all DHT concepts, algorithms, blockchain integration points, and real-world applications to help students see how these pieces fit together in decentralized systems.
